assertion_results.text: The plan explicitly references reusing common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter for comma-separated multi-value query parameter parsing rather than writing new parsing logic (constraint 5.4)
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 16: 'No new parsing logic is needed here — the raw query parameter string (e.g., "MIT,Apache-2.0") is passed to the service layer, which delegates comma-separated parsing to apply_filter from common/src/db/query.rs.' Also line 25: 'use common::db::query::apply_filter to parse the comma-separated string and generate the SQL IN clause condition. This reuses the exact same mechanism the advisory module uses for severity filtering — apply_filter already handles splitting on commas, trimming whitespace, and producing the appropriate SeaORM condition.'

assertion_results.text: The plan references following the filter pattern from modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs as a structural guide for implementing the license filter
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 14: 'following the same pattern used for the severity field in modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs'. Also the Reuse Strategy table in plan.md lists 'modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs' with 'Followed as structural template for adding the optional field'. reuse-analysis.md Section 2 states: 'The advisory list endpoint is used as a structural template — not copied verbatim, but followed step-by-step'.

assertion_results.text: The plan references using entity/src/package_license.rs for the JOIN query rather than writing raw SQL or creating a new entity
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 26: 'Join through the entity::package_license entity (from entity/src/package_license.rs) to connect packages to their license records. Use the existing SeaORM entity relation rather than writing raw SQL or creating a new entity.' Also the Reuse Strategy table lists 'entity/src/package_license.rs' with 'Used as SeaORM entity for the join rather than raw SQL'.

assertion_results.text: The reuse analysis covers all 3 Reuse Candidates from the task description, explaining how each would be applied
assertion_results.evidence: reuse-analysis.md covers all 3 candidates with detailed explanations: Section 1 covers 'common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter' (lines 1-11), Section 2 covers 'modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs' (lines 13-22), Section 3 covers 'entity/src/package_license.rs' (lines 24-30). Each section includes 'What it provides', 'How it is reused', and 'Why reuse over alternatives/new code' subsections.

assertion_results.text: All files listed in the plan are within the scope defined by the task's Files to Modify and Files to Create sections (constraint 5.1)
assertion_results.evidence: plan.md lists exactly 3 files: Files to Modify: 'modules/fundamental/src/package/endpoints/list.rs' and 'modules/fundamental/src/package/service/mod.rs'. Files to Create: 'tests/api/package_license_filter.rs'. The Scope Verification section (lines 58-62) explicitly confirms all files are within scope. No out-of-scope files are mentioned.

assertion_results.evidence: plan.md line 48: 'This implementation creates no new utility functions for query parameter parsing or SQL clause generation. All parsing and filtering logic is handled by existing infrastructure'. The plan consistently references calling apply_filter directly rather than creating any wrapper or new utility function.
